description: ATLS primary survey, hemorrhagic shock, tension pneumothorax, C-spine clearance, FAST exam, damage control

questions.vignette: A 35-year-old unrestrained driver, GCS 9, bilateral equal breath sounds, BP 70/40, HR 130.
questions.prompt: What is the correct ATLS primary survey sequence?

options.B: Airway with C-spine protection, Breathing, Circulation, Disability, Exposure

correct.explanation: ATLS: ABCDE — Airway (with C-spine protection), Breathing, Circulation, Disability, Exposure. Airway obstruction kills fastest. Despite hypotension, airway takes priority because resuscitation requires a patent airway.
correct.mechanism: ATLS rationale: Airway obstruction kills in minutes, tension pneumothorax in minutes, hemorrhagic shock in minutes-hours. C-spine paired with airway because manipulation risks cervical injury.

distractors.A: Circulation before Airway violates ATLS. Airway kills faster than hemorrhage.
distractors.C: Breathing before Airway could miss obstruction. Must ensure patent airway first.
distractors.D: Starting with Disability skips the three most immediately lethal categories.

correct_pathway.question: Explain why 'Airway with C-spine protection, Breathing, Circulation, Disability, Exposure' is the best answer.
correct_pathway.expected_response: ATLS: ABCDE — Airway (with C-spine protection), Breathing, Circulation, Disability, Exposure. Airway obstruction kills fastest. Despite hypotension, airway takes priority because resuscitation requires a patent airway.

correct_pathway.question: What is the underlying mechanism?
correct_pathway.expected_response: ATLS rationale: Airway obstruction kills in minutes, tension pneumothorax in minutes, hemorrhagic shock in minutes-hours. C-spine paired with airway because manipulation risks cervical injury.

A.question: You chose 'Circulation, Airway, Breathing, Disability, Exposure'. Walk me through your reasoning.
A.expected_response: Circulation before Airway violates ATLS. Airway kills faster than hemorrhage.

A.question: Now consider 'Airway with C-spine protection, Breathing, Circulation, Disability, Exposure'. What evidence supports this instead?
A.expected_response: ATLS: ABCDE — Airway (with C-spine protection), Breathing, Circulation, Disability, Exposure. Airway obstruction kills fastest. Despite hypotension, airway takes priority because resuscitation requires a patent airway.

C.question: You chose 'Breathing, Airway, Circulation, Disability, Exposure'. Walk me through your reasoning.
C.expected_response: Breathing before Airway could miss obstruction. Must ensure patent airway first.

C.expected_response: ATLS: ABCDE — Airway (with C-spine protection), Breathing, Circulation, Disability, Exposure. Airway obstruction kills fastest. Despite hypotension, airway takes priority because resuscitation requires a patent airway.

D.question: You chose 'Disability (GCS), Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Exposure'. Walk me through your reasoning.
D.expected_response: Starting with Disability skips the three most immediately lethal categories.

D.expected_response: ATLS: ABCDE — Airway (with C-spine protection), Breathing, Circulation, Disability, Exposure. Airway obstruction kills fastest. Despite hypotension, airway takes priority because resuscitation requires a patent airway.

questions.vignette: A 28-year-old woman with stab wound. BP 100/70, HR 115, RR 24, anxious, UOP 15 mL/hr. Est. blood loss 1.5L.

correct.explanation: Class III: 1.5L (~30%), tachycardia >120, tachypnea, anxiety/confusion, decreased UOP (15-20 mL/hr), SBP beginning to drop. Class III is FIRST class where BP drops — I and II maintain normal SBP through compensation.
correct.mechanism: Classification: I (<750mL): normal vitals. II (750-1500mL): tachycardia, normal BP. III (1500-2000mL): hypotension begins, confusion, UOP 5-15. IV (>2000mL): severe hypotension, obtundation.

distractors.A: Class I has nearly normal vitals. This patient has tachycardia, dropping BP, decreased UOP.
distractors.B: Class II maintains normal BP and UOP >20 mL/hr. This patient's BP is dropping.
distractors.D: Class IV has severe hypotension, obtundation, negligible UOP. Patient is still conscious.

correct_pathway.question: Explain why 'Class III — 30-40% loss' is the best answer.
correct_pathway.expected_response: Class III: 1.5L (~30%), tachycardia >120, tachypnea, anxiety/confusion, decreased UOP (15-20 mL/hr), SBP beginning to drop. Class III is FIRST class where BP drops — I and II maintain normal SBP through compensation.

correct_pathway.question: What is the underlying mechanism?
correct_pathway.expected_response: Classification: I (<750mL): normal vitals. II (750-1500mL): tachycardia, normal BP. III (1500-2000mL): hypotension begins, confusion, UOP 5-15. IV (>2000mL): severe hypotension, obtundation.

A.expected_response: Class I has nearly normal vitals. This patient has tachycardia, dropping BP, decreased UOP.

A.expected_response: Class III: 1.5L (~30%), tachycardia >120, tachypnea, anxiety/confusion, decreased UOP (15-20 mL/hr), SBP beginning to drop. Class III is FIRST class where BP drops — I and II maintain normal SBP through compensation.

B.question: You chose 'Class II — 15-30% loss'. Walk me through your reasoning.
B.expected_response: Class II maintains normal BP and UOP >20 mL/hr. This patient's BP is dropping.

B.question: Now consider 'Class III — 30-40% loss'. What evidence supports this instead?
B.expected_response: Class III: 1.5L (~30%), tachycardia >120, tachypnea, anxiety/confusion, decreased UOP (15-20 mL/hr), SBP beginning to drop. Class III is FIRST class where BP drops — I and II maintain normal SBP through compensation.

D.question: You chose 'Class IV — >40% loss'. Walk me through your reasoning.
D.expected_response: Class IV has severe hypotension, obtundation, negligible UOP. Patient is still conscious.

D.question: Now consider 'Class III — 30-40% loss'. What evidence supports this instead?
D.expected_response: Class III: 1.5L (~30%), tachycardia >120, tachypnea, anxiety/confusion, decreased UOP (15-20 mL/hr), SBP beginning to drop. Class III is FIRST class where BP drops — I and II maintain normal SBP through compensation.

questions.vignette: A 40-year-old MVC patient. Tracheal deviation right, absent left breath sounds, distended neck veins, hypotension. SpO2 78%.
questions.prompt: What is the MOST appropriate immediate action?

options.A: Needle decompression at 2nd ICS, midclavicular line, left

correct.explanation: Classic tension pneumothorax: absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation AWAY from affected side, JVD, hypotension. Clinical diagnosis — do NOT wait for imaging. Immediate needle decompression converts tension to simple pneumothorax.
correct.mechanism: One-way valve: air enters pleural space but can't exit → progressive pressure → lung collapse → mediastinal shift → cardiovascular collapse. Needle decompression releases trapped air.

distractors.B: NEVER delay treatment for imaging. Patient will die waiting.
distractors.C: Positive pressure ventilation without decompression WORSENS tension.
distractors.D: Tamponade has bilateral diminished heart sounds, not unilateral absent breath sounds.

correct_pathway.question: Explain why 'Needle decompression at 2nd ICS, midclavicular line, left' is the best answer.
correct_pathway.expected_response: Classic tension pneumothorax: absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation AWAY from affected side, JVD, hypotension. Clinical diagnosis — do NOT wait for imaging. Immediate needle decompression converts tension to simple pneumothorax.

correct_pathway.question: What is the underlying mechanism?
correct_pathway.expected_response: One-way valve: air enters pleural space but can't exit → progressive pressure → lung collapse → mediastinal shift → cardiovascular collapse. Needle decompression releases trapped air.

B.question: You chose 'Chest X-ray to confirm'. Walk me through your reasoning.
B.expected_response: NEVER delay treatment for imaging. Patient will die waiting.

B.question: Now consider 'Needle decompression at 2nd ICS, midclavicular line, left'. What evidence supports this instead?
B.expected_response: Classic tension pneumothorax: absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation AWAY from affected side, JVD, hypotension. Clinical diagnosis — do NOT wait for imaging. Immediate needle decompression converts tension to simple pneumothorax.

C.question: You chose 'Endotracheal intubation'. Walk me through your reasoning.
C.expected_response: Positive pressure ventilation without decompression WORSENS tension.

C.question: Now consider 'Needle decompression at 2nd ICS, midclavicular line, left'. What evidence supports this instead?
C.expected_response: Classic tension pneumothorax: absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation AWAY from affected side, JVD, hypotension. Clinical diagnosis — do NOT wait for imaging. Immediate needle decompression converts tension to simple pneumothorax.

D.question: You chose 'Pericardiocentesis'. Walk me through your reasoning.
D.expected_response: Tamponade has bilateral diminished heart sounds, not unilateral absent breath sounds.

D.question: Now consider 'Needle decompression at 2nd ICS, midclavicular line, left'. What evidence supports this instead?
D.expected_response: Classic tension pneumothorax: absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation AWAY from affected side, JVD, hypotension. Clinical diagnosis — do NOT wait for imaging. Immediate needle decompression converts tension to simple pneumothorax.

questions.vignette: A 25-year-old alert, sober patient after low-speed rear-end collision. No neck pain, no midline tenderness, no distracting injuries, full ROM. GCS 15.
questions.prompt: Can C-spine be cleared clinically per NEXUS criteria?

options.A: No — all trauma patients need imaging
options.D: Yes — meets all NEXUS criteria for clinical clearance

correct.explanation: NEXUS criteria for clearance WITHOUT imaging: (1) No midline tenderness, (2) No focal deficit, (3) Normal alertness (GCS 15), (4) No intoxication, (5) No distracting injury. All five met. NEXUS: 99.6% sensitivity.
correct.mechanism: NEXUS validated in 34,069 patients, identified 818/818 significant injuries. Identifies low-risk patients avoiding unnecessary CT radiation and cost.

distractors.A: Imaging all trauma patients is unnecessary. Clinical decision rules safely identify low-risk patients.
distractors.B: Low-speed mechanism in alert non-tender patient has extremely low C-spine injury risk.
distractors.C: Flex-ext X-rays are for suspected ligamentous injury WITH tenderness, not NEXUS-negative patients.

correct_pathway.question: Explain why 'Yes — meets all NEXUS criteria for clinical clearance' is the best answer.
correct_pathway.expected_response: NEXUS criteria for clearance WITHOUT imaging: (1) No midline tenderness, (2) No focal deficit, (3) Normal alertness (GCS 15), (4) No intoxication, (5) No distracting injury. All five met. NEXUS: 99.6% sensitivity.

correct_pathway.question: What is the underlying mechanism?
correct_pathway.expected_response: NEXUS validated in 34,069 patients, identified 818/818 significant injuries. Identifies low-risk patients avoiding unnecessary CT radiation and cost.

A.question: You chose 'No — all trauma patients need imaging'. Walk me through your reasoning.
A.expected_response: Imaging all trauma patients is unnecessary. Clinical decision rules safely identify low-risk patients.

A.question: Now consider 'Yes — meets all NEXUS criteria for clinical clearance'. What evidence supports this instead?
A.expected_response: NEXUS criteria for clearance WITHOUT imaging: (1) No midline tenderness, (2) No focal deficit, (3) Normal alertness (GCS 15), (4) No intoxication, (5) No distracting injury. All five met. NEXUS: 99.6% sensitivity.

B.question: You chose 'No — mechanism mandates CT'. Walk me through your reasoning.
B.expected_response: Low-speed mechanism in alert non-tender patient has extremely low C-spine injury risk.

B.question: Now consider 'Yes — meets all NEXUS criteria for clinical clearance'. What evidence supports this instead?
B.expected_response: NEXUS criteria for clearance WITHOUT imaging: (1) No midline tenderness, (2) No focal deficit, (3) Normal alertness (GCS 15), (4) No intoxication, (5) No distracting injury. All five met. NEXUS: 99.6% sensitivity.

C.question: You chose 'Yes, after flexion-extension X-rays'. Walk me through your reasoning.
C.expected_response: Flex-ext X-rays are for suspected ligamentous injury WITH tenderness, not NEXUS-negative patients.

C.question: Now consider 'Yes — meets all NEXUS criteria for clinical clearance'. What evidence supports this instead?
C.expected_response: NEXUS criteria for clearance WITHOUT imaging: (1) No midline tenderness, (2) No focal deficit, (3) Normal alertness (GCS 15), (4) No intoxication, (5) No distracting injury. All five met. NEXUS: 99.6% sensitivity.

questions.vignette: A hypotensive trauma patient with stab wound. FAST exam performed.
questions.prompt: Which space is NOT assessed in the standard 4-view FAST?

correct.explanation: Standard FAST 4 views: (1) RUQ/Morrison's, (2) LUQ/splenorenal, (3) Suprapubic/pelvis, (4) Subxiphoid/pericardial. Left paracolic gutter is NOT a standard view. Positive FAST in hypotensive patient → emergent laparotomy.
correct.mechanism: FAST detects fluid in dependent spaces. Morrison's pouch is most dependent space in supine patient and most sensitive for hemoperitoneum. Takes 2-3 minutes.

distractors.A: Morrison's pouch is standard FAST view — most sensitive location for free fluid.
distractors.C: Splenorenal recess is the standard LUQ view.
distractors.D: Pericardial view is the fourth FAST view, screening for tamponade.

correct_pathway.question: Explain why 'Left paracolic gutter' is the best answer.
correct_pathway.expected_response: Standard FAST 4 views: (1) RUQ/Morrison's, (2) LUQ/splenorenal, (3) Suprapubic/pelvis, (4) Subxiphoid/pericardial. Left paracolic gutter is NOT a standard view. Positive FAST in hypotensive patient → emergent laparotomy.

correct_pathway.question: What is the underlying mechanism?
correct_pathway.expected_response: FAST detects fluid in dependent spaces. Morrison's pouch is most dependent space in supine patient and most sensitive for hemoperitoneum. Takes 2-3 minutes.

A.question: You chose 'Hepatorenal recess (Morrison's pouch)'. Walk me through your reasoning.
A.expected_response: Morrison's pouch is standard FAST view — most sensitive location for free fluid.

A.question: Now consider 'Left paracolic gutter'. What evidence supports this instead?
A.expected_response: Standard FAST 4 views: (1) RUQ/Morrison's, (2) LUQ/splenorenal, (3) Suprapubic/pelvis, (4) Subxiphoid/pericardial. Left paracolic gutter is NOT a standard view. Positive FAST in hypotensive patient → emergent laparotomy.

C.question: You chose 'Splenorenal recess'. Walk me through your reasoning.
C.expected_response: Splenorenal recess is the standard LUQ view.

C.question: Now consider 'Left paracolic gutter'. What evidence supports this instead?
C.expected_response: Standard FAST 4 views: (1) RUQ/Morrison's, (2) LUQ/splenorenal, (3) Suprapubic/pelvis, (4) Subxiphoid/pericardial. Left paracolic gutter is NOT a standard view. Positive FAST in hypotensive patient → emergent laparotomy.

D.question: You chose 'Pericardial space'. Walk me through your reasoning.
D.expected_response: Pericardial view is the fourth FAST view, screening for tamponade.

D.question: Now consider 'Left paracolic gutter'. What evidence supports this instead?
D.expected_response: Standard FAST 4 views: (1) RUQ/Morrison's, (2) LUQ/splenorenal, (3) Suprapubic/pelvis, (4) Subxiphoid/pericardial. Left paracolic gutter is NOT a standard view. Positive FAST in hypotensive patient → emergent laparotomy.

questions.vignette: A 30-year-old with multiple GSWs. 10 units pRBC, temp 34°C, pH 7.18, diffuse oozing.
questions.prompt: What is the MOST appropriate surgical strategy?

options.A: Abbreviate: pack abdomen, temp close, ICU for resuscitation (damage control)
options.B: Continue definitive repair of all injuries
options.C: Close definitively and transfuse until coagulopathy resolves
options.D: Place wound vac, return to OR in 1 week

correct.explanation: Lethal triad: hypothermia (34°C), acidosis (pH 7.18), coagulopathy (diffuse oozing). Continuing surgery = death from irreversible coagulopathy. Damage control: control hemorrhage/contamination, temp close, ICU resuscitation, return to OR 24-48 hours.
correct.mechanism: Lethal triad: hypothermia → impaired clotting → continued hemorrhage → worsening acidosis → worsening hypothermia. Breaking the cycle requires stopping surgery, warming, correcting acidosis, reversing coagulopathy.

distractors.B: Continuing definitive repair in coagulopathic patient → death from uncontrollable hemorrhage.
distractors.C: Definitive closure impossible with coagulopathy. Abdomen will swell → compartment syndrome.
distractors.D: One week too long. Return in 24-48 hours. Prolonged open abdomen increases infection/fistula risk.

correct_pathway.question: Explain why 'Abbreviate: pack abdomen, temp close, ICU for resuscitation (damage control)' is the best answer.
correct_pathway.expected_response: Lethal triad: hypothermia (34°C), acidosis (pH 7.18), coagulopathy (diffuse oozing). Continuing surgery = death from irreversible coagulopathy. Damage control: control hemorrhage/contamination, temp close, ICU resuscitation, return to OR 24-48 hours.

correct_pathway.expected_response: Lethal triad: hypothermia → impaired clotting → continued hemorrhage → worsening acidosis → worsening hypothermia. Breaking the cycle requires stopping surgery, warming, correcting acidosis, reversing coagulopathy.
